result:
Subject: Yesterday's payments outage — what happened and what we're doing about it

Yesterday, between [start] and [end] UTC, our payments service was down for roughly four hours. During that window, some of you saw transactions fail outright — card charges that didn't complete, subscription renewals that errored out, and checkouts that hung or rolled back. We know that wasn't just inconvenient; for many of you, it meant lost sales, frustrated customers of your own, and time spent cleaning up. We're sorry. You trusted us with a critical part of your business, and we let you down.

The root cause was a misconfigured cache invalidation rule that, under a traffic spike, caused our payment authorization service to repeatedly evict and re-fetch hot keys from the database. This cascaded into connection pool exhaustion and, ultimately, service-wide timeouts. The bad rule shipped through our normal review process — our tests didn't exercise it under realistic load, so we didn't catch it before it reached production.

We've rolled back the change and rewritten the invalidation logic with explicit rate limits and a safe fallback path. More importantly, we're changing how we ship: going forward, any change to the caching or data layer goes out via canary rollout to a small slice of traffic first, with automatic rollback on error-rate regressions. We're also adding chaos testing in staging — deliberately inducing traffic spikes, cache failures, and dependency slowdowns — so we catch this class of failure before it ever reaches you.

As a small acknowledgment, we're applying a goodwill credit to every account that had a failed transaction during the incident window. You'll see it on your next invoice, with no action required. If you have follow-up questions or want to talk through impact on your specific account, reply to this email and we'll get back to you directly. Thank you for your patience, and again, we're sorry.

— [Name], [Title]
